subtopics: Grid DFS $\cdot$ Multi-source BFS $\cdot$ Cycle Detection $\cdot$ BFS on Graphs $\cdot$ Memoised DFS $\cdot$ Weighted Graph BFS

sections.title: Grid DFS: Islands and Flood Fill

blocks.content: **Number of Islands.** Given a 2-D binary grid of \texttt{'1'} (land) and \texttt{'0'} (water), return the number of islands. An island is a maximal connected component of land (4-directionally connected).
**Binary Tree Level-Order Traversal.** Return the level-order traversal of a binary tree's node values (level by level, left to right).

**Rotting Oranges.** A grid contains \texttt{0} (empty), \texttt{1} (fresh orange), or \texttt{2} (rotten orange). Each minute, every fresh orange 4-adjacent to a rotten one becomes rotten. Return the minimum minutes until all oranges are rotten, or $-1$ if impossible.

sections.title: Graph DFS: Cycle Detection and Boundary Marking

blocks.content: **Course Schedule.** Given $n$ courses and a list of prerequisites \texttt{[a, b]} (take $b$ before $a$), return \texttt{True} iff you can finish all courses (i.e.\ the prerequisite graph has no cycle).
**Surrounded Regions.** Given a board of \texttt{'X'} and \texttt{'O'}, capture all \texttt{'O'} regions not connected to the border by flipping them to \texttt{'X'}.
**Pacific Atlantic Water Flow.** Water can flow to the Pacific (top/left border) and Atlantic (bottom/right border). Return all cells from which water can reach *both* oceans.
blocks.solution:
**Reverse BFS from both borders simultaneously:** instead of checking each cell's flow, reverse the direction — ask which cells can be *reached* from each ocean by flowing uphill.

\begin{lstlisting}
from collections import deque

def pacificAtlantic(heights: list[list[int]]) -> list[list[int]]:
    rows, cols = len(heights), len(heights[0])

    def bfs(starts):
        visited = set(starts)
        q = deque(starts)
        while q:
            r, c = q.popleft()
            for dr, dc in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]:
                nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
                if (0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols
                        and (nr, nc) not in visited
                        and heights[nr][nc] >= heights[r][c]):
                    visited.add((nr, nc))
                    q.append((nr, nc))
        return visited

    pacific  = [(0, c) for c in range(cols)] + [(r, 0) for r in range(rows)]
    atlantic = [(rows-1, c) for c in range(cols)] + [(r, cols-1) for r in range(rows)]

    return [[r, c] for r, c in
            bfs(pacific) & bfs(atlantic)]  # intersection
\end{lstlisting}

**Complexity:** $O(R\cdot C)$ time and space.

**Key insight:** reversing the flow direction (uphill from ocean) and taking the *intersection* of reachable sets is cleaner and more efficient than simulating downhill flow from each cell.

sections.title: BFS on Word/Graph Problems and Memoised DFS

blocks.content: **Word Ladder.** Given \texttt{beginWord}, \texttt{endWord}, and a \texttt{wordList}, return the length of the shortest transformation sequence from \texttt{beginWord} to \texttt{endWord} (changing one letter at a time, each intermediate word in the list).
**Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix.** Return the length of the longest strictly increasing path in a matrix (4-directional movement).
**Evaluate Division.** Given equations $a/b = k$ and queries $p/q$, return the value of each query using the given ratios (or $-1$ if not computable).
